title_en: Climate Risk Visualizations That Travel Better on Social Platforms
title_zh: Climate Risk Visualizations That Travel Better on Social Platforms
abstract_en: The authors compare multiple graphic styles and caption strategies for climate-risk communication in feed-based environments. They find that high-share designs tend to simplify causal language, foreground practical consequences, and reduce visual clutter while preserving enough scientific credibility to support trust.
abstract_zh: The authors compare multiple graphic styles and caption strategies for climate-risk communication in feed-based environments. They find that high-share designs tend to simplify causal language, foreground practical consequences, and reduce visual clutter while preserving enough scientific credibility to support trust.
plain_summary_zh: This paper asks a practical question: what kind of climate-risk graphic travels best on social platforms? By comparing different visual styles and captions, the authors find that being clear and shareable can matter more than looking maximally technical.
plain_summary_en: This paper asks a practical question: what kind of climate-risk graphic travels best on social platforms? By comparing different visual styles and captions, the authors find that being clear and shareable can matter more than looking maximally technical.
research_direction_zh: It belongs to the design side of science communication, showing how scientific information gets reshaped for platform-based circulation.
research_direction_en: It belongs to the design side of science communication, showing how scientific information gets reshaped for platform-based circulation.
raw_title: Climate Risk Visualizations That Travel Better on Social Platforms
raw_abstract: The authors compare multiple graphic styles and caption strategies for climate-risk communication in feed-based environments. They find that high-share designs tend to simplify causal language, foreground practical consequences, and reduce visual clutter while preserving enough scientific credibility to support trust.
